name: Cedar Creek and Trout Creek
short_description: Free BLM rockhounding near Oliverville where Cedar Creek and Trout Creek expose colorful agate and jasper in gravels and creek beds. No permit required, 25 lb daily limit.
description:
Cedar Creek and Trout Creek offer excellent free rockhounding opportunities on Montana BLM land in scenic Mineral County. These productive creek systems have exposed rich deposits of agate, jasper, and chalcedony in stream gravels and cutbanks, making them accessible for both beginners and experienced collectors. The streams flow through mixed conifer and riparian habitat typical of western Montana's transition zone between the Flathead National Forest and lower elevation valleys.

The geology here reflects the Precambrian metamorphic basement rocks and Tertiary sedimentary sequences characteristic of the Mission Mountains region. Hydrothermal alteration along the creek corridors has produced colorful agate nodules and banded jasper specimens that weather out of hillside exposures and concentrate in active stream channels. Water action continuously exposes fresh material, making each season productive.

Collecting is straightforward - walk the creekbeds and gravel bars, examine cutbanks where water has already done the excavation work, and scan the surrounding hillsides. The streams are typically accessible year-round, though water levels and footing conditions vary seasonally. Personal use collection is free with no permit required under FLPMA regulations, though you must respect the 25 lb daily limit and leave no motorized equipment behind.

This is an ideal location for families new to rockhounding because access is easy, material is abundant, and you can spend as much or as little time as you wish. The combination of water access, exposed geology, and proximity to town makes Cedar Creek and Trout Creek a reliable choice for a productive day of collecting.
history: Cedar Creek and Trout Creek lie within the Mission Mountains metamorphic complex, where Precambrian basement rocks were intruded by Tertiary-age hydrothermal systems. These hot-fluid events deposited silica-rich minerals including agate and jasper along fracture zones and in favorable host rocks, which subsequent erosion and stream action has exposed and concentrated in modern creek gravels.
tips: Focus your search on the active creek channel and recent gravel bars where water has naturally concentrated heavier specimens - agate and jasper tend to accumulate in inside bends and behind obstructions. Examine the lower 2-3 feet of exposed cutbanks where stream erosion reveals fresh material; polished specimens often show color and pattern more clearly when wet. Bring a small classifier or sieve to process stream gravels efficiently, and work early morning or after rain when light is best for spotting translucent agate specimens. Always walk carefully on wet rocks and slippery banks - sturdy boots with good grip are essential.
terrain_notes: Moderate elevation (around 3,500-4,000 feet) riparian terrain with active creek channels flanked by willows and cottonwoods. Immediate creek environment is rocky and potentially slippery with uneven footing. Surrounding hillsides have sagebrush and sparse conifer cover with loose rock common. Walking distance from parking is typically short (under 0.5 mile to productive areas), but terrain demands careful foot placement and sturdy footwear.
best_season: April - October. Water levels are manageable in spring and early summer (May-June) after snowmelt, with good exposures. Late summer (July-September) offers lower water levels and easier creek access. Early spring (April) and fall (October) are pleasant but water can be cold and heavy.
written_directions: From Oliverville, head north on MT 200 for approximately 8-10 miles toward the Mineral County area. Watch for BLM access signs or established pullouts near Cedar Creek and Trout Creek crossings. Local knowledge and county maps are essential - contact the Missoula BLM Field Office for current access points and parking areas, as creek access can shift seasonally. GPS coordinates: 47.173847, -114.878276 will guide you to the general vicinity.
road_conditions: Variable. Access roads may be unpaved and can be rough after rain or snowmelt. High-clearance vehicle recommended but standard passenger vehicles may manage in dry conditions.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts and parking areas near creek access points. No developed parking lot - expect small gravel or dirt areas suitable for 1-4 vehicles. Capacity is limited; arrive early during peak season.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA regulations. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or heavy equipment. Respect all property boundaries and BLM closure notices. Pack out all trash.
rules:
Personal use only - no commercial collection
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No motorized excavation or heavy equipment
Respect BLM closure notices and property boundaries
Pack out all trash
Leave rockhounding site in natural condition
nearest_services: Oliverville, approximately 8-10 miles south. Limited services - fuel, food, and supplies in larger towns like Superior (15-20 miles) or Missoula (30+ miles).
accessibility_notes: Rocky creek terrain with slippery footing on wet rocks and uneven gravel bars. No wheelchair access to productive collecting areas. Sturdy waterproof boots with good grip essential. Hillside terrain around creeks is uneven with loose rock. Not suitable for visitors with limited mobility or balance issues, but manageable for most hiking-capable individuals.

faq.question: What gemstones can I find rockhounding at Cedar Creek and Trout Creek near Oliverville?
faq.answer: Cedar Creek and Trout Creek are known for colorful agate, jasper, chalcedony, and quartz. Specimens range from small tumbling-grade pieces in stream gravels to larger nodules in hillside exposures. Most collectors find polished agate with red, orange, and white banding, plus translucent chalcedony suitable for lapidary work.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Cedar Creek and Trout Creek in Montana?
faq.answer: No permit is required. These are Montana BLM lands open to recreational collecting under the Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA). Personal use collection is free, though you must follow the 25 lb daily limit and cannot use motorized equipment.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to collect gemstones at Cedar Creek and Trout Creek?
faq.answer: April through October is ideal, with May-June offering excellent access after spring snowmelt exposes fresh material in stream channels. July-September provides lower water levels and easier footing. Avoid late fall and winter when creek crossings become hazardous and weather is unpredictable.

faq.question: Is Cedar Creek and Trout Creek rockhounding site family-friendly and suitable for beginners?
faq.answer: Yes, it's an excellent beginner and family site. Access is easy with short walks to productive gravel bars, abundant material makes finding specimens straightforward, and no permit or fees apply. Children age 6+ can participate safely with adult supervision and proper footwear. The free BLM access and high success rate make it ideal for first-time rockhounders.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do I need to collect agate and jasper at Cedar Creek and Trout Creek?
faq.answer: A small shovel or trowel, sieve or classifier for processing stream gravels, sturdy waterproof boots, and a collection bag are essential. A geology hammer is useful for breaking open nodules, but motorized equipment is not permitted. Bring water, sun protection, and a GPS device or map since cell service is spotty.

faq.question: How far is Cedar Creek and Trout Creek from Oliverville and what are the road conditions?
faq.answer: The site is approximately 8-10 miles north of Oliverville via MT 200. Access roads are unpaved and can be rough, especially after rain or snowmelt. A high-clearance vehicle is recommended; contact the Missoula BLM Field Office for current road and parking conditions. Informal pullouts serve as parking areas near productive creek sections.
